sentences.text: What you need is a little piece of paper.
You know, if you heat this up a little bit and you push it together-- but you gotta be careful.
It's not the--
No, No, it's this.
It's this.
It spread.
One piece is not from the other one.
Yeah.
Strasnoy? ?] Did you hear [?
Strasnoy? ?]
Yeah.
Strasnoy ?] is from [?
Nasdrich. ?]
I don't know if he is from [?
Nasdrich ?] but his wife is a cousin of mine.
He used to be also a builder.
I'm fine, thank you.
My name is Leon Flumenbaum.
L-E-O-N, Capital F-L-U-M-E-N-B-A-U-M.
I was born April 13, 1914 in the city of Zwolen, not far from Radom, Poland.
My parents-- my father's name was Moshe.
My mother's name was [?
Ruchla. ?] Her maiden name was Kuperberg.
My father was born 1985.
1885, I stand corrected.
And my mother was born 1897.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She is the daughter of Shmuel Chaim Kuperberg and Yochevet Kuperberg, from home Weintraub.
My grandfather's name, after whom I am called, was Leibish Flumenbaum.
And my grandmother's name was Hinda [?
Hirschman, ?] and she comes from Lublin.
All the rest are born and lived in Zwolen.
Brothers-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I'm the oldest.
And after me was Irving.
He was born in 1916.
After Irving came Sam, 1919, and Paul, 1922.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a little sister, which was born 1931-- December 22, 1931.
I had an older sister by the name of Hinda, which was not from my mother.
It was from my father's first wife, which her name was Nacha and she was an elder sister to my mother.
And she died at the time of giving birth to her daughter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was married, had a husband by the name of Moshe Szlaferman.
Had two kids.
Moshe Szlaferman was born in 1903.
My sister was born 1912.
And son, Wolf [?
Zaiv, ?] was born 1931.
And a daughter, Nacha, she was born in 1933.
They all vanished in the Holocaust.
In Zwolen before the war, my father was a tailor.
He had a shop in the house.
He employed a few people, working for us.
He was a good salesman, and life wasn't too bad.
I never had the-- a feeling of hunger or being not clothed well.
All of our children went to school.
All finished public school.
There was no high school in our city, and we couldn't afford to send anybody to a different city to study in the high school or something like this.
It was very costly.
So they settled.
My sister became the treasurer of the bank and I started off as a freeloader, helping out in the house.
And then by selling, going to the markets with the products they used to produce in our house.
And I've been in the business with my father.
Later on, I turned a little bit political.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became a member of the Zionist movement.
I was about 11 years of age.
A Zionist.
I didn't like the conditions under which Jews that used to live in Poland at that time.
And I was thinking really of going to Israel and build a home for Jews.
And in this way, I joined the Zionist movement.
I became Hashomer.
And at 15, I became the secretary of the grand treasurer of the [INAUDIBLE].
This was an office given to me by all parties concerned, which was the Betal, was the [?
Sim ?] Klahim, [INAUDIBLE], Mizrahi.
And they choose me to be the secretary, which I holded till 1934.
Keren Kayemeth.
That's JNF.
Yeah.
And this-- later on when I grow older, the Zionist-- the Zionist organization divided.
More radical, more left.
Things that I belonged, I turned into the Hashomer Hatzair.
And I became the leader of Hashomer Hatzair.
And then in 1934 I decided to do my bit [INAUDIBLE], and I went to a kibbutz.
First I went to Radom.
You're still in Poland?
In Poland.
Poland, Poland.
This is--
Kibbutz is Hachshara.
This is a preparedness place, where they prepared the Jews-- the Jewish boys--
For Aliyah.
To work hard and all kind of things.
I started with Radom.
Then, we joined another group, which was Chelm.
I went to Chelm.
And finally we wind up in Lublin, because the kibbutz in Lublin went to Israel and we took their place in Lublin.
Lublin was a big city, and Lublin was a great kibbutz.
We had a lot of wealth, established places, work places, where we could make a living.
And we stayed there until I was approved for Aliyah to Israel.
I am now 82.
Yesterday.
Then?
I was-- 1935 I was 20 years of age, about 22 years of age when I was approved.
But to my sorrow, I didn't have enough money to pay for the certificate and the traveling expenses.
I had to go home and work to make money for the cost of Aliyah.
And meanwhile, the war broke out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before you get into the war, what type of religious education did you get?
Cheder.
Until-- until about 13 years of age.
Until I was bar mitvah.
I-- I know Hebrew very well.
I loved [INAUDIBLE] and [?
Gamurah-- ?] All kind of things.
And I was pretty knowledgeable in it.
In elementary school.
But later on in the years, especially in the kibbutz, we had a-- a top school.
We learned about life, literature and everything what a-- a young man should know about life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I brought myself up to a certain higher standard of educational level.
And I lived with it, and I worked this way.
Self-educated.
Before the Germans came, I was in the city, working as a painter.
I tried to learn a trade which was good for building a country.
And I was working. 1st of September 1939, the war broke out.
I was home.
Then our city was bombed, and we left the city and went to-- with my future brother-in-law's.
He had knowledge of the territory around the city and had a lot of Poles, which he used to deal with.
So we went to one of his comrades, and we stayed there a few days until the Germans settled in the city.
We sent my little sister, with his daughter, to find out what's what.
So, as it was, they came back and they said, you know, we've seen the Jews marching and singing the [?
Mikvah. ?] She thought that they were.
So she said the [?
Mikvah. ?] And we laughed.
We had a laugh, but after 10 days in the woods, we went home.
As we approached our city, the Germans were waiting for us.
All youngsters they called to go to work, to clean up the city from the bombs, shells, from bricks-- clean the city.
And then every single day, they used to take us to go on the road-- fix the roads.
I especially had no luck to them and every day I was called by them.
At one time-- and this went on so close to three months.
Every morning I had to go to work.
They brought us back at night.
And one time I was pushing a wheel-- a wheel cart on top of a one board to a machine to turn over the-- the stones for them to break it.
I lost my balance and fell down.
So the German hit me and-- in my right side, and I don't hear in my ear until today.
And then I decided to go away.
And coming home, I told my parents that I decided this is my decision.
If they want, let's go together but they didn't.
And three other friends of mine-- we're four-- and we started off to go to the side of the Soviet Union, to the part where the Russians were.
Then after a week or so of traveling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came about the border-- the new border with the new [INAUDIBLE] and the controlled part of the Germans.
While we were approaching the Bug-- it was the River Bug-- four Germans came up on horses, and they caught us four.
They brought us back to the city.
It was a Saturday morning, and people going to the shul yet then, they thought that this is the finish.
They went [INAUDIBLE] for us, but somehow we had documents, secure documents from the German authority.
We had a neighbor who became the Burgermeister.
This is the-- the-- the mayor of the city, Zwolen, who was living next to us.
And he gave us-- nobody should know-- papers that we are from Rowne.
We're going back home.
They respected it and let us out.
Went back to the border, and at that night we paid off some [INAUDIBLE] and it took us over by-- the other side of the river.
And over there started the journey to hide from the Russians, otherwise, they will send us back.
And we-- we made it.
That night probably we made about 60, 70 miles walking.
I had on my body-- I had an overcoat, three pair of underwear, three pair of shirts.
Everything I possessed, was on me.
This was the part of the year, the end of November 1939.
And we reached Lublin, the first city on the Ukrainian side.
We came into Lublin.
I had an address of a Jewish couple whose family lived in our city.
And I was very close with them.
[DRINKS] We looked them up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this lady was very nice to us.
She warmed up water.
We all washed and went to bed.
We slept all day long there.
She fed us, and the next day, we thanked her and went to our destinations.
I picked-- I-- my destination was Rowne.
Because in Rowne I had cousins living.
The uncle wasn't alive.
My aunt was alive yet.
And this is a story with this family.
My uncle used to be a banker before the war, before the Revolution.
He lived in [INAUDIBLE] in Ukraine.
And after the Revolution, they had to flee Russia.
So they came.
They stayed with us in our house almost two years.
We slept all together on the floor-- and everywhere there was a place we were together.
So I felt that I have a home to go to.
And I came there, believe it or not-- the cousin of mine wasn't home.
He was a big [? seucher ?] and he wasn't home.
But his family-- his wife-- had [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She picked her hands and said, oh, thank God.
Somebody of you is here.
And they treated me well but, look, after all I wasn't a-- a kid anymore.
I was a young man.
And over there I found a full house of people from family-- cousins, first cousins, second cousins.
All day it was about 15 people there from Warsaw, from Radom, from [INAUDIBLE].
The whole family was there.
So the next day, I went to a different cousin's house, and I was there.
And then-- and I lived in Rowne, until in January-- this was the beginning of December.
In January, the Russians started to register people to go to the Soviet Union.
For labor-- to work, to live there because here, the conditions were terrible.
All the shuls, all of the public places, were taken from the [?
Biegenses ?], from the people who came from the other side, which ran away from the part when Germany took over.
The railroad station-- it's a big beautiful, railroad station-- you couldn't walk down the steps-- every piece was taken by people sitting there, and sleeping-- and doing everything what they needed.
It was terrible.
So, knowing that I have an uncle in Moscow, living before the war-- so I thought, look, maybe I'll go there and it'll ease up a little bit.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was thinking about it.
Then, when they used to start to call meetings in the main movie house, in the theater house, this and that, and they pleaded with-- with us, please, go register.
Go away to the Soviet Union.
The war just started.
We don't know what tomorrow will bring.
You are not hosted.
You are strangers to us.
So please, avoid things which will be not pleasant for you.
Go there and live together with our people.
So they warned you?
They warned us.
I don't say no.
They warned us.
So a group from our city-- you have a picture here from them-- we decided to go to one room, from our [INAUDIBLE] and we stayed there.
The day when they supposed to send us away, they came there.
They took us all at one time.
They put us in cattle wagons, and they sent us off.
And to-- we wind up in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The conditions?
We had straw in one side.
We had to sleep on it.
The other side was a pail-- things like this, for water-- things like this.
It was livable.
They didn't shove you all together.
No, no, livable.
And they took us to [?
Calcasia. ?] We passed [?
Rastov. ?] We passed [?
Calcasia ?].
And on the main station-- railroad station-- they stopped the whole echelon.
It was a lot of wagons.
And one of the commissars came over and he told, this group goes here.
This group goes here.
This group goes here.
I, with my group, wind up in a [INAUDIBLE].
This was a government property, and we were sent, twenty-some people there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was about 50 miles from a railroad station.
They had about 50,000, 60,000 sheep.
Over there they used to grow sheep for the Karakul-- for the-- for wool, number one.
And besides, they used to kill the little sheep when they were a day old, a few hours old, for the-- to make the fur.
The-- and it's the very best.
Breitschwanz and the Karakul.
This what they used to do.
And they had a lot of land.
And we supposed to work there and make a living.
They gave us quarters to live in.
They treated us nice.
I can't complain.
We lived the same way as their own people lived, and we worked.
We worked.
They had machineries.
They had all kind of tools and stuff to be repaired.
And we acquainted ourselves with the things, and we used to do it.
In fact, one of our young people-- the youngest from us-- he became a specialist in making all of the cylinders by the tractors and the cups by the machines.
He used to put on the lye on it, and then work it around to set it in.
And they didn't know what to do with him.
He was a top mechanic.
And we lived.
During the time, I tried to connect my uncle.
In Moscow?
In Moscow.
And through the help of the director of the school over there-- he wrote the letters for me-- I didn't know Russian at that time.
And one day, I received a telegram from my uncle, telling me that he sees that somebody from his family is there.
I should let them know who I am.
So I went to this director and he wrote a letter, and I told him who I am.
So I received a letter that now you can take off.
But in November, there is a holiday of the Russian Revolution.
So then he can take up who was coming for me to see.
And this was-- was November 6 he came, 1940.
He came to see me, to look me up.
And somehow, he had a lot of-- his brother-in-law was one of the [INAUDIBLE] in the Newspaper?
Newspaper.
Lieberman, ?] his wife's brother.
And they had gotten a letter to allow me to go to Moscow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they freed me there, and I came to Moscow December 24, 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I stayed in Moscow almost three months, till March, but I couldn't remain in Moscow because I was not a citizen of the-- of the Soviet Union.
So, he made up with them that I'm going to Berdichev.
Because the second part of the [INAUDIBLE] which remained in the [INAUDIBLE], during this time, went to Berdichev.
They moved to Berdichev.
They moved to Berdichev so I went to Berdichev with them.
And Berdichev-- I came in March and June 22, the war broke out.
So by that time, I was working.
They called it [INAUDIBLE].
This was a tremendously big factory.
They made their machines, all kind of turbines.
Very big.
Heavy machinery.
Heavy machinery.
They built their machines, which cleaned the-- the gasoline to be able to use for-- for airplanes.
Refineries.
Refineries.
[INAUDIBLE] refineries, like.
And I was working on the connection of the railroad from the main line to the factory.
So I was working there.
But when the war-- war broke out, we all evacuated.
The whole machine, with everything in it, they evacuated.
I never seen in my lifetime so much silver blocks which was there, bricks of silver, which they use there in the factories.
And with all these goods, we were evacuating.
And the evacuation was a very hard, hard work because we were bombed all the time.
Our [?
Shalom ?] was bombed, and many of times we were laying outside, there [?
Shalom ?] on the sides where the river used to go-- things like this-- to hide ourselves, not to be killed.
And this is the way it went down.
Other [?
Shalom ?] supposed to go to Sebastopol on the Crimea.
And before we came to Crimea, Crimea was taken by the Germans.
So we remained in no man's land.
We didn't know where to go.
